title: Peanut butter fudge
subtitle: American-style cooked fudge with smooth peanut butter
excerpt: Sugar, milk, and butter cooked to soft-ball stage, then beaten with peanut butter as it cools to develop a smooth, dense grain. Richer and less crumbly than chocolate fudge, with a clean peanut flavour throughout.
sourceNotes: Peanut butter fudge is an American confection that became widespread in the early twentieth century as peanut butter moved into domestic use. The cooking technique (soft-ball stage, 112-116°C, with beating on cooling) follows the same British cooked-fudge method documented by Mrs Beeton and Eliza Acton but adds peanut butter as the flavour base. The American tradition of fudge-making is particularly associated with college campuses from the 1880s onward.

recipe.yieldDescription: about 600 g (36 pieces from a 20 cm square tin)
recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cut pieces between sheets of baking paper. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight container at room temperature for 2 weeks.

glossaryTerms.definition: The confectionery temperature stage reached when sugar syrup hits 112-116°C. A drop in cold water forms a soft ball that flattens when pressed. Fudge cooked to this stage and beaten while cooling develops a fine crystal structure; cooked above it and the texture shifts towards toffee.

content.text: Peanut butter fudge uses the same cooked-fudge method as chocolate fudge: cook to

and beat on cooling. The peanut butter goes in just before beating and melts through the hot mixture evenly, seeding the fudge with fat that keeps the texture dense and smooth rather than crumbly. Use smooth peanut butter rather than crunchy for a uniform grain.
Sugar syrup at 116°C causes serious burns. Use a heavy-based pan and keep the handle steady when stirring. If hot syrup contacts skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes.

Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Put the {{granulated-sugar}}, {{whole-milk}}, and {{unsalted-butter}} in a medium heavy-based saucepan. Stir over a low heat until the sugar dissolves and the butter melts. Bring to a boil and cook to 112-116°C (soft-ball stage), stirring occasionally to prevent catching. This takes 15-18 minutes.

Remove from the heat and stir in the {{smooth-peanut-butter}} and {{vanilla-extract}} until fully melted and combined. Leave the pan to cool, undisturbed, until the fudge is warm but not hot (around 60°C, roughly 15 minutes). Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon for 5-8 minutes until the mixture thickens, loses its gloss, and starts to look grainy at the edges. Pour immediately into the lined tin, smooth the top, and leave to set at room temperature for at least 2 hours before cutting.
